### Utilized Software

**LLMs & orchestration**
- **LLMs** — provider-agnostic via LangChain supports **OpenAI (GPT)**, **Anthropic (Claude)**, and **Google Gemini** (both AI Studio API-key and Vertex AI routes). The provider/model is a runtime config switch (`LLM_PROVIDER`/`LLM_MODEL`), not hardcoded — we ran with Gemini during development. Every stage has a deterministic based on heuristics which is keyless offline fallback in case of retry LLM call failures, so the pipeline is fully reproducible without any LLM as well.
- **LangGraph** — state-machine orchestration across all four layers: a Supervisor + Swarm pattern for the literature-mining agents, a ReAct-style designer for the surface/inhibitor selection agent, and a bounded Reflection loop that closes the validation cycle.
- **LangChain** — model abstraction, structured-output parsing, and the scholarly source clients.

**Atomistic / scientific computing**
- **ASE** (Atomic Simulation Environment) — slab construction, adsorption geometry search, structure I/O.
- **MACE** (`mace-torch`) — the foundation machine-learning interatomic potential (MLIP) used for Tier-1 adsorption-energy calculations on real 3D slabs; **CHGNet** and **torch-dftd** (D3 dispersion) as alternate/supplementary MLIP backends; **PyTorch** as the compute backend (CPU/CUDA).
- **RDKit** — builds real 3D inhibitor/precursor molecules from SMILES (ETKDGv3 embedding + MMFF).
- **pymatgen** — generates the crystalline-derived amorphous slabs (SiO₂, Si₃N₄).
- **tblite** (GFN2-xTB) — semi-empirical Tier-2 spot-checks that calibrate the MLIP energies against a cheaper independent method.

**Data, knowledge graph & delivery**
- **NetworkX** — the typed knowledge graph (surfaces, inhibitors, precursors, mechanisms, citations) that Layer 1 builds and every later layer queries.
- **Pydantic** — schema validation across every inter-layer JSON artifact, so hand-offs between layers are typed and self-checking.
- **httpx** — clients for the scholarly literature sources (arXiv, OpenAlex, Crossref, PubMed, Semantic Scholar) that ground every hypothesis in real, DOI-cited literature.
- **Matplotlib** — the figure suite (selectivity curves, growth curves, adsorption-energy bars, site-density bars, and rendered atomic-model slab/molecule views).
- **LaTeX** (IEEEtran class, compiled with **TeXLive** via `latexmk`/`pdflatex`) — the autonomously authored manuscript.
- **Docker**  — For Orchestration of the pipeline which is fully potable and reproducible 

**Techniques**

![AS-ALD Co-scientist architecture](data:image/png;base64,)
- A **four-layer agentic funnel**: literature-grounded hypothesis generation → human-in-the-loop commitment → tiered in-silico validation → autonomous manuscript authoring.
- **Fidelity-gated amorphous surface generation** — slabs are rejected outright if their per-site-type densities fall outside published (Kim et al. 2026) experimental bands, before any expensive reactivity compute is spent.
- **Site-matched (not strongest-binder) inhibitor screening** — a three-step protocol scoring candidates by how well they passivate the precursor's actual reactive sites.
- **Tiered compute with literature calibration** — cheap Tier-0 literature-prior screens promoted to real Tier-1 MLIP calculations (and optional Tier-2 xTB cross-checks) only for promising/committed candidates, with every predicted energy compared against a literature anchor and flagged if it diverges.
- **Bounded Reflection loop** — a closed-loop refine/accept cycle with a fixed iteration budget, so the agent can revise a rejected hypothesis without running indefinitely.
- **A LangGraph swarm of section-writer agents** authors the IEEE-format manuscript itself: each section is drafted in parallel, grounded strictly in the run's own JSON artifacts (no invented numbers), with deterministic fallbacks if the LLM output fails validation.

**Runtime & compute-optimization parameters**

Every cost lever is a runtime env/UI switch (nothing hardcoded), so a run can be tuned from a ~20-minute demo to a multi-hour high-fidelity campaign on the same image:

- **One-click runtime profiles** (A100-calibrated; each bundles all the levers below):
  - *Fast (< 30 min)* — Standard sampling, ensemble 1, melt/quench 800/1500 steps, autotune off.
  - *Balanced (< 1 hr)* — Standard sampling, ensemble 2, melt/quench 1500/3000.
  - *High-fidelity (> 1 hr)* — High-GPU sampling, ensemble 3, melt/quench 3000/8000, MD autotune on.
- **Tier-1 GPU sampling presets** — the levers that actually load the GPU, since MACE evaluates one structure at a time through ASE (a small slab never saturates an A100): supercell size × adsorption sites × rotations × heights. *Standard* (2×2, 4 sites, 4 rot, 2 heights) → *High* (3×3, 8, 6, 3) → *Max* (4×4, 12, 8, 5).
- **`MLIP_DISPERSION` (D3 on/off)** — the torch-dftd D3 step roughly doubles per-eval time; disabling it is **≈2× faster** screening with largely unchanged ranking (exposed as a "disable D3" toggle).
- **`SCREEN_WORKERS`** — candidate-parallel screening on the shared slabs; each worker gets its own **thread-local** MACE calculator (ASE calculators cache results on the instance, so they cannot be shared across threads). On a single GPU expect **~2–3× (not linear)**.
- **Screening-funnel knobs** — `SCREEN_POOL_SIZE` (40, range 10–50), `SCREEN_SHORTLIST_M` (10), `SCREEN_TOP_K` (3), `SCREEN_ENSEMBLE_N` (2 during the batch, full ensemble for the top-k), `SCREEN_RESERVE_NOVEL_FRAC` (0.5), `SCREEN_GENERATIONS` (1 = off; >1 = bounded closed loop).
- **Melt-quench MD** (`SLAB_SOURCE=md-amorphous`) — `MQ_MELT_TEMPERATURE_K` (3500), `MQ_MELT_STEPS`/`MQ_QUENCH_STEPS` (3000/8000), `MQ_TIMESTEP_FS` (0.5, NVT/fixed-cell — foundation MLIPs give unphysical densities under NPT), `MQ_FIX_BOTTOM_FRAC` (0.4 bulk anchor), plus an optional **LLM param-autotuner** (`MQ_AUTOTUNE`) that tunes melt-T/quench on a cheap probe slab scored by the fidelity gate.
- **`MLIP_DEVICE`** (auto/cuda/mps/cpu) — MACE energy *differences* require **float64**, which the MPS backend cannot do, so it auto-falls back; `TORCHDYNAMO_DISABLE=1` avoids the compile mega-cache re-registration error.
- **`MAX_VALIDATION_ITERS`** — the bounded reflection budget (default 2). Long runs survive UI disconnects (detached worker thread + log-tail reattach by run-id) and write artifacts to persistent `/data` when a volume is attached.

**Compute tiers & their limitations**

- **Tier 0 — literature / heuristic priors** (CPU, keyless, seconds). Adsorption energies come from literature anchors and functional-group ↔ site rules. *Limits:* not computed on the real geometry; purely **comparative ranking**, not a quantitative claim; AI-proposed novel compounds have no priors and are flagged `extrapolated=True`, so they can never be reported as "supported" on Tier-0 alone.
- **Tier 1 — foundation MLIP (MACE-MP)** (GPU/CPU). Real 3D RDKit molecules on real pymatgen-derived slabs; `dE = E(slab+mol) − E(slab) − E(mol)`. *Limits:* foundation-MLIP accuracy, **not DFT**; energies are only meaningful as **differences within one fixed calculator + head + dispersion + dtype** (never mix `mace_mp()` / `MACECalculator()`); **reaction barriers are the weakest quantity** (arXiv:2502.15582 caveat); one-structure-at-a-time throughput; float64-only (no MPS); search coverage bounded by sites × rotations × heights.
- **Tier 2 — GFN2-xTB spot-check** (tblite). Recomputes a small subset (`XTB_SPOTCHECK_SITES`, default 1) of Tier-1 configs as an **independent** cross-check feeding the `calibration_vs_literature` block. *Limits:* xTB is **not ground truth** — it is a red-flag detector; a large MLIP-vs-xTB disagreement means the MLIP energies are untrustworthy for that system, nothing more.

Everything degrades gracefully: if the MLIP stack is absent the engine falls back to Tier-0; if `tblite` is absent the spot-check is skipped; every LLM stage has a keyless deterministic fallback.

**Agents**

- **Inhibitor-proposer agent** — a ReAct-style *generative* agent that **invents new** small-molecule inhibitor candidates rather than ranking a fixed library. It reads the Layer-1 knowledge graph (mined mechanisms, functional-group ↔ site rules, prior verdicts) and emits `{name, smiles, functional_group, target_surface, expected_dE_range, rationale, citations}`. Two interchangeable paths on the same schema: **LLM** (real key) or an **offline combinatorial generator** (10 volatile backbones × 5 selective head groups = up to 50 keyless candidates). It repairs LLM SMILES (`Si → [Si]`, Al/Ti/Zr/Hf/… bracketing) and RDKit-validates them, so silane / high-k proposals survive while chemically invalid valences are dropped. In the closed loop it receives **site-resolved feedback** (per-candidate ΔE on the non-growth vs growth surface, chemisorb/physisorb regime, and a directive to spare the growth surface's –OH sites). All its outputs are tagged `provenance='ai-proposed'`, `extrapolated=True` — they must be proven on the real slabs.
- **Screening agent (funnel)** — replaces one-candidate-per-iteration with a batch campaign: **pool** (N = 10–50; designer library + proposer fill) → **Tier-0 prior-rank all N** (honest, no committed-candidate pin) → **MLIP batch-screen the shortlist M on *shared* gated slab ensembles** (identical geometry → apples-to-apples) → **full-fidelity re-run of the global top-K** at the configured tier (xTB cross-check at Tier ≥ 2) → **RecommendationAgent** (winner + runners-up + risks). The shortlist **reserves a fraction for AI-proposed novels** so prior-rank cannot bury them, and **closed-loop generations** feed a failed batch's site-resolved results back to the proposer to design and re-screen a new generation on the same slabs — **until the target is met or the budget is spent** (bounded, never infinite). The winner's rich results are restored to `asald_results.json` for the Layer-4 deep-dive, with the full campaign table in `screening_results.json`.
